encodeMessage.name: Encode a message
encodeMessage.description: Write an encode function that shifts each letter in a message by a given amount. Spaces should remain as spaces. Letters that shift past 'z' should wrap around to the beginning of the alphabet.

caesarSimpleShift.name: Simple shift by 1
caesarSimpleShift.description: Shift each letter forward by 1: a->b, b->c, c->d.

caesarShiftBy3.name: Shift by 3
caesarShiftBy3.description: Shift each letter forward by 3: h->k, e->h, l->o, o->r.

caesarWrapAround.name: Wrap around the alphabet
caesarWrapAround.description: When shifting goes past 'z', it wraps around: x->a, y->b, z->c.

caesarWithSpaces.name: Message with spaces
caesarWithSpaces.description: Spaces should remain as spaces, only letters are shifted.

caesarRot13.name: ROT13 encryption
caesarRot13.description: ROT13 is a special case of the Caesar cipher with a shift of 13.

shiftingLetterByN.question: How do I think about shifting a letter by N positions?
shiftingLetterByN.answer: Each letter has a position in the alphabet (a=1, b=2, and so on). To shift, you find that position, add the shift amount, then convert back into a letter. Writing helpers for 'letter to position' and 'position to letter' will keep things tidy.

letterPosition.question: How do I find the position of a letter in the alphabet?
letterPosition.answer: Write a small helper that loops through the alphabet string until it finds the letter, returning the index when it does. The reverse helper does the opposite, taking a position and returning the letter at that index.

wrapPastZ.question: What happens when the shift goes past 'z'?
wrapPastZ.answer: It needs to wrap around back to 'a'. The modulo operator (`%`) is perfect for this. Taking the position modulo 26 brings any number back into the 0 to 25 range.

applyToWholeMessage.question: How do I apply this to a whole message?
applyToWholeMessage.answer: Loop through each character of the message, shift it using your single-letter helper, and use concatenation (`+`) to build up the result string from the shifted letters.
